questions.question: On a radiographic report, which word commits you to a rejection?
options: Indication
Discontinuity
Defect
Density variation
questions.explanation: A defect is, by definition, a discontinuity that fails the applicable acceptance criteria - it is rejectable. Indication and discontinuity carry no accept/reject verdict at all.

questions.question: Interpretation and evaluation are distinct steps in reading a radiograph. Which pairing is correct?
options: Interpretation compares against code limits; evaluation explains what caused the indication
Interpretation determines what produced the indication; evaluation compares the discontinuity against acceptance criteria
The two terms mean the same thing in radiography
Evaluation is performed first, then interpretation confirms it
questions.explanation: Interpretation sorts indications into false, nonrelevant, and relevant by establishing the cause; evaluation then measures a relevant discontinuity against the governing acceptance criteria.

questions.question: Which list contains only causes of FALSE indications?
options: Backing ring image, weld spatter, surface grinding marks
Crimp marks, static discharge, processing streaks
Slag inclusions, porosity, undercut
Pitting, fatigue cracking, erosion
questions.explanation: False indications originate on the film itself - crimp marks, static, pressure marks, and processing streaks. Backing rings and spatter are nonrelevant (real part features), and the other lists are real discontinuities.

questions.question: A two-inch elongated slag inclusion passes under one fabrication code and is rejected under another. What explains this?
options: The films must have been processed to different densities
One of the readers misidentified the discontinuity
Defect status depends on the governing acceptance criteria, which differ between codes
Slag inclusions change size between viewings
questions.explanation: Acceptance criteria belong to the contract, not the flaw. The same documented discontinuity can be acceptable under one code's limits and a defect under another's - which is why the report must name the criteria used.

questions.question: Why do fabrication codes typically reject cracks and incomplete fusion at any size?
options: They are always deeper than the film suggests
Sharp planar discontinuities concentrate stress far out of proportion to their volume
They cannot be repaired economically
They always grow during hydrostatic testing
questions.explanation: A sharp-edged planar flaw acts as a severe stress concentrator and a ready fatigue-crack starter, so codes reject them outright regardless of length, unlike rounded volumetric discontinuities that are tolerated within limits.

questions.question: A ragged, branching dark line at a section change in a casting, formed while the metal was still hot and restrained by the mold, is a:
options: Cold shut
Hot tear
Quench crack
Forging burst
questions.explanation: Hot tears form during solidification when the weak, still-hot metal is torn by restrained contraction, typically at section changes - ragged and branching, unlike the smooth rounded line of a cold shut.

questions.question: Which group consists entirely of processing discontinuities?
options: Seam, forging lap, quench crack
Ingot pipe, segregation, blowholes
Fatigue crack, pitting, creep voids
Cold shut, hot tear, shrinkage
questions.explanation: Seams and laps come from primary forming and quench cracks from heat treatment - all processing. The second and fourth groups are inherent, and the third is service-induced.

questions.question: Bright, light (low film density) spots in the image of a GTAW weld most likely indicate:
options: Clustered porosity
Tungsten inclusions - denser than steel, so they absorb more of the beam
Burn-through at the root
Crater cracks
questions.explanation: Tungsten is far denser than steel, attenuates more radiation, and prints as a bright light spot - the one common weld discontinuity that appears light rather than dark.

questions.question: Corrosion under insulation is suspected on an operating line that cannot be stripped. The radiographic technique of choice is:
options: Contact film shots after removing all insulation
A profile (tangential) shot imaging the pipe wall edge-on through the insulation
A double-wall double-image elliptical exposure
Fluorescent-screen real-time viewing at the boundary
questions.explanation: Profile radiography shoots tangentially across the pipe so the wall appears edge-on through the insulation, allowing remaining thickness to be measured on an operating line without stripping it.

questions.question: At an outage, a weld discontinuity documented as acceptable on the original construction radiographs is found to have grown. The correct Level II action is to:
options: Reject the weld - any growth automatically makes it a defect
Accept it - it passed evaluation at construction
Report the change against the baseline film and evaluate it under the applicable in-service or fitness-for-service criteria
Re-rate the line so the discontinuity falls within limits
questions.explanation: Service findings are judged against the rule book that governs in-service inspection - typically fitness-for-service methods - using the retained construction films as the baseline. Growth demands re-evaluation, not an automatic verdict.
